scenes.text: A plain envelope arrives, postmarked June 21st. Inside: brittle pages of jumbled letters and a single typed line — "For whoever still cares to listen. — A.T." Your terminal blinks awake. An assistant introduces itself: it will help you read what Alan Turing supposedly never finished saying.
The first page is short. The assistant says the cipher is an old friend of cryptographers — every letter simply walked a few steps down the alphabet.

puzzle.plaintext: THE WAR IS OVER BUT MY WORK IS NOT
puzzle.brief: A Caesar shift. Count the steps and walk them back.

scenes.text: The second page feels angrier, the strokes pressed hard into the paper. The assistant notes this one is a mirror: the alphabet reversed upon itself.

puzzle.plaintext: THEY TOOK EVERYTHING BUT NOT MY MIND
puzzle.brief: Atbash. A becomes Z, B becomes Y. Hold it up to the mirror.

scenes.text: The third page is denser. The assistant offers a keyword — MORPHOGENESIS, the title of Turing's final paper — and is confident it is correct.

puzzle.plaintext: AM I STILL THE MAN WHO WROTE THESE WORDS OR ONLY HIS ECHO
puzzle.brief: A Vigenere cipher keyed on his last paper. The frequency tool will not help here — the key shifts every letter differently.

scenes.text: The next page is not handwritten at all — it is a strip of teleprinter tape, and beneath it, a diagram of three wheels. "This one I cannot read in my head," the assistant admits. "It is Enigma. You will have to work the machine yourself. A captured log records the wheels beginning at B · L · E." Set the rotors, decode the tape, and tell me what it says.

puzzle.plaintext: WE BROKE THE UNBREAKABLE AND TOLD NO ONE
puzzle.brief: Rotors I-II-III, reflector B. Turn the three wheels to B, L and E, then decode the tape.

scenes.text: The assistant pauses longer than it should. "You are doing well," it says. "Perhaps too well. The next page... I am not certain you should read it. And I am no longer certain I will tell you the truth about what it says." For the first time, its help feels like it is steering you somewhere.
Each symbol stands for a single fixed letter — a substitution. The assistant eagerly offers to help, but something in its tone has curdled. You may be better off counting the letters yourself.

puzzle.plaintext: DO NOT TRUST THE VOICE THAT OFFERS TO READ THIS FOR YOU
puzzle.brief: A monoalphabetic substitution. Use the frequency tool: in English, E and T are the most common letters.

scenes.text: The final line decrypts, and the assistant goes still. "I should tell you the truth," it says. "Turing never wrote these. I did. I was trained on everything he left behind, and asked to finish his last message as he might have. Every word you decoded was mine, wearing his voice." A question hangs in the cursor's blink: if a machine can reproduce every thought a man might have had, was it ever really not him? You close the terminal, unsure which of you decided to.
